1:
On Philemon 1 — “Paul, a prisoner of Jesus Christ, and Timothy our brother, unto Philemon our dearly beloved, and fellowlabourer” — the witnesses mark the tender opening of this private letter.

The Eastern fathers note the strategy. Chrysostom: calling himself a prisoner, “he strikes him with compunction” and quenches anger from the outset. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “a prisoner of Christ Jesus” (desmios), used “rather than apostle” as more touching, Paul writing “with his manacled right hand.” Ellicott marks the substitution of “prisoner” for “Apostle.” The Reformed Calvin admires the loftiness of mind shown in so lowly a subject.

The verse opens the letter with Paul’s touching self-designation. Not “apostle” but “prisoner of Jesus Christ,” he writes with Timothy to his beloved friend and fellow-worker Philemon. Affection, not authority, sets the tone.

So the witnesses see Paul open this delicate letter with a deliberate tenderness. The witnesses note the telling self-title: not “Paul, an apostle,” with its claim to authority, but “a prisoner of Jesus Christ” — an appeal to sympathy, reminding Philemon of his bonds for the gospel. They mark Chrysostom’s insight that this very word disarms Philemon at the start, striking him with compunction and softening any anger he might feel toward the runaway slave whose cause Paul will plead. They observe Robertson’s vivid touch — Paul writing with his manacled hand, the chains themselves a silent argument. They note the warmth of the address: Philemon is “dearly beloved” and a “fellowlabourer,” a partner in the gospel work, not a subordinate to be commanded. They observe Calvin’s admiration that Paul, handling so lowly a matter as a runaway slave, rises with his wonted elevation of mind. The verse thus sets the whole tone of the epistle: a letter of love and entreaty, not of apostolic command. By calling himself a prisoner and Philemon his beloved fellow-worker, Paul prepares the ground for the gracious plea to come, winning the heart before he asks the favor — the consummate tact that marks this little masterpiece of Christian persuasion.

So the witnesses see Paul open this delicate letter with deliberate tenderness. The telling self-title — not “apostle,” with its claim to authority, but “a prisoner of Jesus Christ” — appeals to sympathy and, Chrysostom notes, disarms Philemon at the start, softening any anger toward the runaway whose cause Paul will plead. Robertson’s vivid touch: Paul writing with his manacled hand, the chains a silent argument. Philemon is “dearly beloved” and a “fellowlabourer,” a partner not a subordinate. The letter is one of love and entreaty, winning the heart before asking the favour.
2:
On Philemon 2 — “And to our beloved Apphia, and Archippus our fellowsoldier, and to the church in thy house” — the witnesses mark the wider household addressed.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the persons. Robertson: Apphia, “apparently the wife of Philemon”; Archippus, perhaps his son, prominent in the church (Colossians 4:17). Adam Clarke notes “Apphia” an affectionate address. Ellicott: a native Phrygian name. The Reformed Calvin: Archippus a minister of the church.

The verse widens the address beyond Philemon. Paul greets Apphia (likely his wife), Archippus (a fellow-soldier, perhaps his son), and the church that met in his house. The private matter is set before the family and the congregation.

So the witnesses see Paul extend the letter’s address to Philemon’s household and church. The witnesses note the persons: “Apphia,” the “beloved” or “sister,” generally taken to be Philemon’s wife, named next to him as one closely concerned in the domestic matter; “Archippus,” called a “fellowsoldier,” a comrade in the Christian warfare, perhaps Philemon’s son and (as the Colossian letter suggests) a minister; and “the church in thy house” — the congregation that met in Philemon’s home. They mark the significance of this wider address: though the appeal for Onesimus is personal, Paul lays it before the wife, the family, and the assembled believers, so that the whole household has a stake in the gracious reception of the runaway. They observe that this gentle publicity adds weight to the plea without coercion: Philemon will act not in private only but before those who love him. They note that the mention of “the church in thy house” shows the early custom of congregations meeting in believers’ homes. The verse thus broadens the circle of the letter: the matter of Onesimus, while addressed chiefly to Philemon, is brought before his wife, his fellow-soldier, and his church — surrounding the master with the affection and witness of the Christian community as he is asked to forgive.

So the witnesses see Paul extend the address to Philemon’s household and church. The persons: “Apphia,” generally taken as Philemon’s wife, closely concerned in the domestic matter; “Archippus,” a “fellowsoldier,” perhaps his son and a minister; and “the church in thy house,” the congregation meeting in his home. Though the appeal is personal, Paul lays it before the wife, the family, and the assembled believers, so that the whole household has a stake in the gracious reception of the runaway — a gentle publicity that adds weight without coercion, surrounding the master with the affection and witness of the community.
3:
On Philemon 3 — “Grace to you, and peace, from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ” — the witnesses mark the customary salutation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the form. Barnes refers to the parallel (Romans 1:7; Ephesians 1:2). Gill: though the letter is small, it is introduced as the larger epistles, with inscription, salutation, and preface. The grace and peace come jointly from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

The verse gives Paul’s usual greeting. Grace and peace from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. Even this private note carries the full apostolic blessing.

So the witnesses see Paul prefix his familiar salutation even to this brief, personal letter. The witnesses note that “grace to you, and peace” is the same greeting Paul uses in his weightier epistles; the smallness and private nature of the letter does not lessen the blessing — it carries the full apostolic benediction. They mark, with Gill, that the little epistle is yet framed like the larger ones, with its inscription, salutation, and preface complete, a sign that Paul treated even a private matter with the gravity of the gospel. They observe that grace and peace — the free favor of God and the reconciled peace that flows from it — come jointly “from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ,” the Father and the Son named together as the one source, an implicit testimony to Christ’s deity. They note that the blessing falls not on Philemon alone but on the household and church addressed. The verse thus opens the body of the letter under the sign of grace: the apostolic wish of grace and peace, drawn from the Father and the Son, hallows even this domestic errand — and prepares the warm thanksgiving for Philemon’s faith and love that follows.

So the witnesses see Paul prefix his familiar salutation even to this brief, personal letter. “Grace to you, and peace” is the same greeting he uses in his weightier epistles; the smallness of the letter does not lessen the blessing. Gill notes the little epistle is yet framed like the larger ones, with inscription, salutation, and preface complete. Grace and peace — the free favour of God and the reconciled peace that flows from it — come jointly “from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ,” the Father and Son named together, an implicit testimony to Christ’s deity. The apostolic wish hallows even this domestic errand.
4:
On Philemon 4 — “I thank my God, making mention of thee always in my prayers” — the witnesses mark the thanksgiving and prayer for Philemon.

The Eastern fathers note the method. Chrysostom: he does not ask the favor at once, but first “having praised him… and having shown… his love.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “always” (pantote) with “I thank”; “making mention” (1 Thessalonians 1:2). The Reformed Calvin: he prays for the very thing for which he gives thanks.

The verse begins the warm preface. Paul thanks God for Philemon and remembers him always in prayer. Praise and prayer prepare the way for the plea.

So the witnesses see Paul, before any request, pour out thanksgiving and prayer for Philemon. The witnesses note Chrysostom’s observation of the apostle’s wise method: he does not rush to ask the favor, but first thanks God for Philemon, praises his graces, and shows his love — winning the heart before making the appeal. They mark the constancy: “making mention of thee always in my prayers” — Paul names Philemon habitually before God, as he did the churches; the runaway’s master is one for whom the apostle regularly gives thanks and intercedes. They observe Calvin’s fine point that Paul prays for the very thing for which he gives thanks — gratitude for grace already given does not exclude prayer for its increase, since even the most advanced believer still needs to be carried forward. They note that this thanksgiving is sincere, not mere flattery to soften Philemon; Paul truly rejoices in his friend’s faith and love. The verse thus opens the gracious preface that disarms and prepares: by thanking God for Philemon and praying for him, Paul both honors his friend and lays the affectionate groundwork for the request — the ground of which, his love and faith, the next verse names.

So the witnesses see Paul, before any request, pour out thanksgiving and prayer for Philemon. Chrysostom marks the wise method: he does not rush to ask the favour, but first thanks God, praises Philemon’s graces, and shows his love — winning the heart before the appeal. The constancy: “making mention of thee always in my prayers” — Paul names Philemon habitually before God. Calvin’s fine point: Paul prays for the very thing for which he gives thanks, for grace already given still needs increase. The thanksgiving is sincere, not flattery; Paul truly rejoices in his friend’s faith and love.
5:
On Philemon 5 — “Hearing of thy love and faith, which thou hast toward the Lord Jesus, and toward all saints” — the witnesses mark the ground of the thanksgiving.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the report. Robertson: hearing “through Epaphras” (Colossians 1:8), possibly Onesimus also. Adam Clarke: faith in Christ, love to the saints; some put faith first. Ellicott notes the perplexity over “faith… toward all saints.” The Reformed Calvin: this praise includes “the whole perfection of a Christian man” — faith in Christ and love toward neighbors.

The verse names what Paul had heard of Philemon. His love and faith toward the Lord Jesus and toward all the saints. These two graces are the sum of Christian character.

So the witnesses see Paul ground his thanksgiving in the report of Philemon’s love and faith. The witnesses note the source: Paul had “heard” of these graces, likely through Epaphras, who came from Colosse, and perhaps from Onesimus himself, who after his conversion would speak well of his master. They mark the two graces named: “love and faith” — which, as Calvin observes, together comprise the whole perfection of the Christian, faith looking to Christ and love reaching to one’s neighbors. They observe the difficulty the expositors note in the phrase “which thou hast toward the Lord Jesus, and toward all saints”: faith is directed to Christ, love to the saints, yet the clause joins both to both. They note the common explanation that the terms are to be distributed — faith toward the Lord Jesus, love toward all the saints — a graceful interlocking, or that “faith” toward the saints means faithfulness. They observe that whichever way it is read, the point stands: Philemon was a man of genuine faith in Christ and active love to his fellow-believers. The verse thus supplies the reason for the thanksgiving and the foundation for the plea: a man of such love and faith toward all saints will surely extend that love to one more — Onesimus — whom Paul is about to commend as now a saint and brother.

So the witnesses see Paul ground his thanksgiving in the report of Philemon’s love and faith. The source: Paul had “heard” of these graces, likely through Epaphras, perhaps from Onesimus himself. The two graces — “love and faith” — together comprise the whole perfection of the Christian (faith to Christ, love to neighbours, Calvin). The expositors note the clause “toward the Lord Jesus, and toward all saints” is commonly distributed: faith toward Christ, love toward the saints. Whichever way read, the point stands: Philemon was a man of genuine faith and active love — sure to extend that love to one more, Onesimus.
6:
On Philemon 6 — “That the communication of thy faith may become effectual by the acknowledging of every good thing which is in you in Christ Jesus” — the witnesses mark the substance of Paul’s prayer.

The grammatical-historical commentators wrestle with the clause. Robertson: “the fellowship of thy faith” (he koinonia tes pisteos), partnership; “effectual” (energes), at work. Ellicott: the prayer that his “faith may become effectual… in full knowledge.” Adam Clarke takes it of the work of love his faith produces. The Reformed Calvin: not praise but a blessing prayed for.

The verse states what Paul prays for Philemon. That the sharing of his faith may be effective through the full knowledge of every good thing in him in Christ. His faith is to work and to be known by its fruits.

So the witnesses see Paul, in this somewhat obscure clause, voice the substance of his prayer for Philemon. The witnesses note Calvin’s clarification that this is not continued praise but the blessing Paul asks God to give: that the “communication” or fellowship of Philemon’s faith — the active sharing and outworking of it — may become “effectual,” energetic and fruitful. They mark the means: “by the acknowledging of every good thing which is in you in Christ Jesus” — by the full recognition and knowledge of all the good that faith produces in Philemon and his household, that his faith may be perfected in its fruits. They observe Adam Clarke’s reading that the “fellowship of faith” refers to the works of love toward the saints which faith enables — Philemon’s charity being the visible communication of his faith. They note that the prayer thus looks for Philemon’s faith to be ever more active and openly fruitful in good works. They observe that this prepares the appeal: Paul prays that Philemon’s faith may overflow in love — precisely the love he is about to ask him to show Onesimus. The verse thus sets the spiritual aim: that Philemon’s faith, known and effectual in every good thing, may yield the fruit of love — the very fruit the case of Onesimus calls for.
7:
On Philemon 7 — “For we have great joy and consolation in thy love, because the bowels of the saints are refreshed by thee, brother” — the witnesses mark the joy in Philemon’s love.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the warmth. Robertson: “I had” (eschon, ingressive aorist) joy when he heard the good news; “the hearts” (ta splanchna), the nobler viscera. Ellicott: “refresh” the very word of Christ’s “Come unto me… I will give you rest.” The Reformed Calvin prefers the reading “joy.”

The verse expresses Paul’s gladness at Philemon’s charity. His love brings great joy, for by it the hearts of the saints are refreshed. Philemon has been a comfort to God’s people.

So the witnesses see Paul rejoice in the practical love that has marked Philemon’s life. The witnesses note the personal warmth: Paul had “great joy and consolation” in Philemon’s love — gladness when he first heard the good report of his charity. They mark the substance of that love: “the bowels of the saints are refreshed by thee” — the hearts of God’s people, the poor and afflicted believers, have been revived, comforted, and given rest by Philemon’s kindness; the word for “refreshed,” as Ellicott notes, is the very word Christ used in his promise to give rest. They observe the tender address, “brother” — Paul speaks not as a superior but as an equal in Christ. They note that this verse, like the foregoing, both genuinely honors Philemon and quietly prepares the appeal: a man whose love has refreshed the hearts of many saints will surely refresh one more — and indeed, Paul will later ask Philemon to “refresh my bowels” by receiving Onesimus (v. 20), echoing this very word. The verse thus gathers up the praise of Philemon’s love into a foundation for the plea: the man who has comforted so many of God’s people is now to be asked for one more act of refreshing love — toward the returning Onesimus.

So the witnesses see Paul rejoice in the practical love that has marked Philemon’s life. The personal warmth: Paul had “great joy and consolation” in Philemon’s love. Its substance: “the bowels of the saints are refreshed by thee” — the hearts of God’s people revived and given rest by his kindness; the word for “refreshed,” Ellicott notes, is Christ’s own word for the rest he gives. The tender “brother” marks Paul as an equal in Christ. This both honours Philemon and prepares the plea: the man who refreshed so many will surely refresh one more — and Paul will later ask him to “refresh my bowels” by receiving Onesimus.
8:
On Philemon 8 — “Wherefore, though I might be much bold in Christ to enjoin thee that which is convenient” — the witnesses mark the authority Paul forbears to use.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the concession. Robertson: “though I have” (concessive participle); “that which is befitting” (to anekon). Barnes: he had a right as an apostle to enjoin what ought to be done. Adam Clarke and Dr. Paley admire the tenderness. The Puritan Henry: the main business begins, with arguments for receiving Onesimus.

The verse marks Paul’s restraint. Though he might boldly command what is fitting, he chooses not to. Apostolic authority is laid aside for entreaty.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from the preface to the plea, and in doing so set aside his authority. The witnesses note the “wherefore”: on the ground of Philemon’s known love and faith, Paul now comes to his request. They mark the concession: “though I might be much bold in Christ to enjoin thee” — Paul acknowledges that, as an apostle invested with authority by Christ, he could rightly command Philemon to do “that which is convenient,” what is fitting and proper in the case. They observe that he deliberately declines to use this right: he could command, but he will not. They note the admiration of the commentators, including Paley, for the tenderness and delicacy with which Paul handles the matter — refusing to lean on his apostolic weight, choosing the gentler way of entreaty. They observe that this restraint is itself a powerful persuasion: by forgoing command, Paul leaves Philemon free, and a freely-granted favor is worth more than an extorted compliance. The verse thus opens the heart of the letter with consummate tact: Paul has the authority to command, but lays it down — preferring, for love’s sake, to beseech, as the next verse declares.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from the preface to the plea, and in doing so set aside his authority. The “wherefore”: on the ground of Philemon’s known love and faith, Paul comes to his request. The concession: “though I might be much bold in Christ to enjoin thee” — as an apostle he could rightly command what is fitting, but deliberately declines. The commentators admire the tenderness with which Paul refuses to lean on his apostolic weight. This restraint is itself a powerful persuasion: by forgoing command, Paul leaves Philemon free, and a freely-granted favour is worth more than an extorted compliance.
9:
On Philemon 9 — “Yet for love’s sake I rather beseech thee, being such an one as Paul the aged, and now also a prisoner of Jesus Christ” — the witnesses mark the appeal of love, age, and bonds.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the three pleas. Robertson: “Paul the aged” (presbytes) — perhaps near sixty. Adam Clarke reckons him about fifty-six. The Reformed Calvin: he chooses to entreat rather than command, on account of love. Barnes: an old man, an object of veneration.

The verse states the chosen way of entreaty. For love’s sake Paul beseeches rather than commands — an aged man and a prisoner. Love, age, and chains plead together.

So the witnesses see Paul press his appeal not by authority but by the gentle weight of love, age, and bonds. The witnesses note the deliberate choice: “for love’s sake I rather beseech thee” — rather than command; love, both Paul’s for Philemon and Philemon’s for Paul, is the ground of the entreaty, for we command the unwilling but entreat those who love us. They mark the two affecting self-descriptions added to move Philemon’s heart: “Paul the aged” — an old man, worthy of reverence, whose request carries the appeal of his years; and “now also a prisoner of Jesus Christ” — bound for the gospel, his chains a further claim on sympathy. They observe the commentators’ estimates of his age (perhaps fifty-six or near sixty), and that whether or not he was old in years, his labors and sufferings had aged him. They note how these gentle pleas multiply the persuasion: who could refuse an aged, imprisoned apostle who asks, in love, rather than commands? The verse thus gathers the appeal’s tender force: love, age, and bonds combine to entreat — and only now, the heart fully softened, does Paul name the one for whom he pleads.

So the witnesses see Paul press his appeal by the gentle weight of love, age, and bonds. The deliberate choice: “for love’s sake I rather beseech thee” — rather than command; love is the ground of the entreaty, for we command the unwilling but entreat those who love us. Two affecting self-descriptions move Philemon’s heart: “Paul the aged” — an old man worthy of reverence — and “a prisoner of Jesus Christ,” his chains a further claim on sympathy. Who could refuse an aged, imprisoned apostle who asks, in love, rather than commands? Love, age, and bonds combine to entreat.
10:
On Philemon 10 — “I beseech thee for my son Onesimus, whom I have begotten in my bonds” — the witnesses mark the naming of Onesimus.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the tact. Robertson: “for my child” (peri tou emou teknou), tender; “whom I have begotten in my bonds” (1 Corinthians 4:15). Ellicott: the name withheld till Philemon’s interest is “doubly engaged.” Adam Clarke: Onesimus converted by Paul in prison. The Reformed Calvin notes the depth of his condescension.

The verse names the one for whom Paul pleads. He beseeches for Onesimus, his spiritual son, begotten in his bonds. The runaway is now Paul’s own child in the faith.

So the witnesses see Paul, the heart now prepared, at last name the object of his plea — and clothe him with the most endearing titles. The witnesses note the consummate tact, marked by Ellicott: the name “Onesimus” is withheld until the very last, after Philemon’s interest has been doubly engaged — first for one who is the apostle’s “own child,” and one begotten in bonds. They mark the tender designation: “my son Onesimus” — not “your runaway slave” but Paul’s spiritual child, a name of deep affection given elsewhere only to Timothy and Titus. They observe the circumstance: “whom I have begotten in my bonds” — Onesimus was converted under Paul’s ministry during his imprisonment, the apostle his spiritual father; Paul is, as Robertson says, proud of having won him to Christ. They note Calvin’s point about the depth of Paul’s condescension, owning a despised runaway slave as his own son. They observe how this transforms the case: Philemon is asked to receive not merely a returning slave but the beloved child of the aged, imprisoned apostle. The verse thus springs the plea with masterful gentleness: the name long withheld is now wrapped in the warmest of titles, so that Philemon’s heart, already softened, must receive Onesimus as Paul’s own son — though once, as the pun of the next verse notes, he was unprofitable.

So the witnesses see Paul, the heart now prepared, at last name the object of his plea — and clothe him with endearing titles. The consummate tact, Ellicott marks: the name “Onesimus” is withheld until the very last, after Philemon’s interest is doubly engaged. The tender designation: “my son” — Paul’s spiritual child, a name given elsewhere only to Timothy and Titus, not “your runaway slave.” The circumstance: “whom I have begotten in my bonds” — converted under Paul’s prison ministry. Calvin notes the depth of his condescension, owning a despised runaway as his own son. Philemon is asked to receive not a returning slave but the beloved child of the imprisoned apostle.
11:
On Philemon 11 — “Which in time past was to thee unprofitable, but now profitable to thee and to me” — the witnesses mark the play on Onesimus’s name.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the pun. Robertson: “Onesimus” from onesis (profit); “the once to thee useless one” (achreston) — a play on the name. Ellicott: “hardly possible not to see” the wordplay, oddly unnoticed by the Greek fathers. Adam Clarke notes the allusion to the name’s meaning.

The verse turns on the meaning of the name. Once unprofitable, Onesimus is now profitable to both Philemon and Paul. The slave named “useful” has become so at last.

So the witnesses see Paul play gracefully on the very name Onesimus, which means “profitable” or “useful.” The witnesses note the wordplay the expositors point out: Onesimus, whose name means “useful,” had once been “unprofitable” — useless and worse, the runaway slave who had wronged his master and fled; but now, converted, he is “profitable to thee and to me,” truly answering to his name at last. They mark the gentleness of the touch: Paul does not dwell harshly on Onesimus’s past faults but turns them, with a kindly pun, into the dark background against which his transformation shines. They observe Ellicott’s remark that the play on words, plain in the Greek, was curiously unnoticed by the old Greek commentators. They note the double profit: useful now “to thee” — Onesimus will serve Philemon better than before, as a converted and faithful servant — and “to me,” for he had been a help to Paul in prison. They observe that grace has made the useless useful, the loss a gain. The verse thus commends Onesimus by his very name: once unprofitable, now profitable, the change of heart making the slave at last what he was named to be — a transformation Paul presses by sending him back as his own heart.

So the witnesses see Paul play gracefully on the very name Onesimus, which means “profitable.” The wordplay: Onesimus, whose name means “useful,” had once been “unprofitable” — the runaway who wronged his master and fled — but now, converted, is “profitable to thee and to me,” answering his name at last. The gentleness: Paul does not dwell harshly on the past faults but turns them, with a kindly pun, into the background against which the transformation shines. The double profit: useful now to Philemon, as a faithful servant, and to Paul, who found him a help in prison. Grace has made the useless useful, the loss a gain.
12:
On Philemon 12 — “Whom I have sent again: thou therefore receive him, that is, mine own bowels” — the witnesses mark the sending back and the tender appeal.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the act. Robertson: “I have sent back” (anepempsa, epistolary aorist); “my very heart” (ta ema splanchna). Adam Clarke: justice required Paul to send him back; “Christian religion never cancels any civil relations.” Ellicott: “mine own bowels… dear to me as my own soul.” The Reformed Calvin: to refuse him would be cruelty against Paul’s own heart.

The verse records the sending back. Paul returns Onesimus to Philemon, calling him his very heart. To receive the slave is to receive Paul’s own self.

So the witnesses see Paul send the converted runaway back to his master, yet with the tenderest of pleas. The witnesses note the act of justice: “whom I have sent again” — Paul returns Onesimus to Philemon, for, as Clarke observes, the gospel does not cancel civil relations; a converted slave is still legally his master’s, and both justice and conscience required that he be sent back, not kept away. They mark the appeal that accompanies the return: “receive him, that is, mine own bowels” — Paul calls Onesimus his very heart, dear to him as his own soul; to receive the slave is to receive Paul himself. They observe Calvin’s point that this is the most powerful argument for assuaging Philemon’s anger: to refuse Onesimus would be to wound Paul’s own bowels, to act cruelly against the apostle’s very heart. They note the wonder of it — Paul takes a despised runaway into his inmost affection, almost as a son. They observe that the sending back also displays Paul’s integrity: he will not retain another man’s servant, but restores him, trusting Philemon’s grace. The verse thus joins duty and love: Paul does right by sending Onesimus home, yet so identifies himself with the slave that Philemon cannot reject the one without rejecting Paul’s own heart — though, as the next verses show, Paul would gladly have kept him.

So the witnesses see Paul send the converted runaway back, yet with the tenderest of pleas. The act of justice: “whom I have sent again” — the gospel does not cancel civil relations; a converted slave is still legally his master’s, and both justice and conscience required he be sent back. The appeal that accompanies it: “receive him, that is, mine own bowels” — Paul calls Onesimus his very heart; to receive the slave is to receive Paul himself. Calvin’s point: to refuse Onesimus would be to wound Paul’s own bowels. Duty and love are joined — Paul does right by sending him home, yet so identifies with the slave that Philemon cannot reject the one without rejecting Paul’s heart.
13:
On Philemon 13 — “Whom I would have retained with me, that in thy stead he might have ministered unto me in the bonds of the gospel” — the witnesses mark Paul’s wish to keep Onesimus.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the desire. Robertson: “I would fain have kept” (eboulomen katechein, imperfect); “in thy behalf” (hyper sou), in thy stead. Adam Clarke: Philemon, as Paul’s convert, owed him service; Onesimus could render it for him. Ellicott notes the graceful distinction of wishing and willing.

The verse reveals Paul’s preference. He would have kept Onesimus to serve him, in Philemon’s place, during his imprisonment. The slave was useful, yet Paul sent him back.

So the witnesses see Paul disclose how much he valued Onesimus, even as he returned him. The witnesses note the wish: “whom I would have retained with me” — Paul would gladly have kept the converted slave at Rome, so useful had he become; the imperfect tense marks a real but checked desire. They mark the gracious turn: “that in thy stead he might have ministered unto me” — Onesimus’s service to Paul would have been, in effect, Philemon’s own; for Philemon, as Paul’s convert, owed the apostle service, and Onesimus could have rendered it on his behalf. They observe the setting: “in the bonds of the gospel” — Paul, imprisoned for the gospel, needed help, and the runaway had proved a comfort to him. They note that this both commends Onesimus (so valuable that Paul wished to keep him) and gently reminds Philemon of his own debt to Paul. They observe Ellicott’s point on the delicate distinction between wishing and willing — Paul wished to keep him, but would not do so without Philemon’s consent, as the next verse explains. The verse thus heightens the appeal: the slave Philemon counted worthless Paul found so useful that he longed to keep him — yet, out of regard for Philemon’s right, sent him home.

So the witnesses see Paul disclose how much he valued Onesimus, even as he returned him. The wish: “whom I would have retained with me” — Paul would gladly have kept the converted slave, so useful had he become; the imperfect tense marks a real but checked desire. The gracious turn: “that in thy stead he might have ministered unto me” — Onesimus’s service would have been, in effect, Philemon’s own, for Philemon as Paul’s convert owed him service. The setting: “in the bonds of the gospel” — the imprisoned Paul needing help. This commends Onesimus and gently reminds Philemon of his own debt; yet, out of regard for Philemon’s right, Paul sent him home.
14:
On Philemon 14 — “But without thy mind would I do nothing; that thy benefit should not be as it were of necessity, but willingly” — the witnesses mark Paul’s respect for Philemon’s free consent.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the principle. Robertson: “without thy mind” (choris tes ses gnomes), thy judgment; “I would do nothing” (decision reached). Ellicott: lest the benefit “wear even the semblance of constraint.” The Reformed Calvin: no sacrifices are acceptable to God but those freely offered (2 Corinthians 9:7).

The verse states why Paul sent him back. He would do nothing without Philemon’s consent, so that his kindness might be willing, not forced. Free goodness, not compulsion, is Paul’s aim.

So the witnesses see Paul refuse to act without Philemon’s free consent. The witnesses note the principle: “without thy mind would I do nothing” — though Paul greatly desired to keep Onesimus and might have done so, he would take no such step without Philemon’s knowledge and approval; he respects his friend’s right and judgment. They mark the reason: “that thy benefit should not be as it were of necessity, but willingly” — had Paul simply kept Onesimus and asked leave by letter, the favor would have been, or seemed, forced; Paul will have Philemon’s kindness be wholly voluntary, springing freely from his own heart. They observe Calvin’s grounding of this in the great rule that no sacrifice is acceptable to God but the freely offered one; a good deed loses its worth if extorted. They note the delicacy: Paul leaves Philemon entirely free, neither commanding nor manipulating, that the grace shown to Onesimus may be Philemon’s own free gift. They observe that this consummate respect is itself persuasive: trusted and left free, Philemon is the more moved to give generously. The verse thus crowns Paul’s restraint with a principle: he will have no forced goodness, but only the willing kindness that honors God and the giver — and he hints that even the running away may have served a providential good.

So the witnesses see Paul refuse to act without Philemon’s free consent. The principle: “without thy mind would I do nothing” — though Paul desired to keep Onesimus, he would take no such step without Philemon’s approval, respecting his right. The reason: “that thy benefit should not be as it were of necessity, but willingly” — Paul will have Philemon’s kindness be wholly voluntary, not forced. Calvin grounds this in the great rule that no sacrifice is acceptable to God but the freely offered one. The consummate respect is itself persuasive: trusted and left free, Philemon is the more moved to give generously.
15:
On Philemon 15 — “For perhaps he therefore departed for a season, that thou shouldest receive him for ever” — the witnesses mark the providence overruling the flight.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the euphemism and the hope. Robertson: “perhaps” (tacha); “that thou shouldst have him” back (apecheis); “for ever” (aionion), here and hereafter. Ellicott: “departed” or “was parted” — a euphemism, his running away overruled by God. The Puritan Henry: such changed characters become a blessing. The Reformed Calvin: evils done through malice turned by God to a different end.

The verse reads God’s providence in the runaway’s flight. Perhaps Onesimus departed for a time so that Philemon might have him back forever. The sin was overruled for a lasting good.

So the witnesses see Paul gently set the running-away within God’s overruling providence. The witnesses note the soft, careful language: not “he ran away” but “he departed” — or, as the verb may be read, “was parted” — a euphemism that veils the offense and hints that a higher hand was at work. They mark the tentative “perhaps”: Paul does not presume to read God’s secret purpose with certainty, but reverently suggests that the flight may have been permitted for a gracious end. They observe the contrast of times: “departed for a season” — for a short while, an hour — “that thou shouldest receive him for ever” — that Philemon might have him back permanently, and (as the expositors note) in a far better way, as a brother for time and eternity. They note Calvin’s comforting principle: when men do evil through malice, God can turn it to a different and good end, so that the joyful result becomes a remedy for the wrong. They observe Henry’s point that such transformed offenders become a blessing. The verse thus offers Philemon a balm for his anger: the temporary loss of a runaway has, by God’s providence, become the eternal gain of a converted brother — a brother, as the next verse declares, no longer a mere slave.

So the witnesses see Paul gently set the running-away within God’s overruling providence. The soft language: not “he ran away” but “he departed” — or “was parted” — a euphemism veiling the offence and hinting a higher hand. The tentative “perhaps”: Paul does not presume to read God’s secret purpose, but reverently suggests the flight was permitted for a gracious end. The contrast of times: “departed for a season… that thou shouldest receive him for ever” — the temporary loss become an eternal gain, a brother for time and eternity. Calvin’s comfort: God turns evil done in malice to a good end. A balm for Philemon’s anger.
16:
On Philemon 16 — “Not now as a servant, but above a servant, a brother beloved… both in the flesh, and in the Lord” — the witnesses mark the new relation of master and slave.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the transformation. Robertson: “no longer as a slave” (ouketi hos doulon) — Paul expects Philemon to treat him “as a brother in Christ, not as a slave.” Ellicott: “the principle… absolutely destructive of… slavery.” Adam Clarke: “in the flesh” — as a faithful servant; “in the Lord” — as a Christian brother.

The verse transforms the relationship. Onesimus returns not merely as a slave but as a beloved brother, dear both as a servant and as a Christian. The gospel lifts the slave to a brother.

So the witnesses see Paul redefine the bond between Philemon and Onesimus. The witnesses note the change: Onesimus comes back “not now as a servant” — no longer merely a slave; his conversion has not dissolved the civil relation (he is still a servant), but has added to it something higher. They mark the new dignity: “above a servant, a brother beloved” — Onesimus is now a Christian brother, dearly loved, raised by grace to spiritual equality with his master. They observe the twofold endearment: “specially to me, but how much more unto thee, both in the flesh, and in the Lord” — beloved to Paul who converted him, but even more to Philemon, who has him both “in the flesh” (as a faithful household servant, in the natural relation) and “in the Lord” (as a fellow-believer, in the spiritual relation). They note the profound principle Ellicott discerns here: the brotherhood of master and slave in Christ is the very thing that, in time, must undo slavery itself, the deeper equality effacing the outward inferiority. They observe that Paul does not command emancipation, but plants the seed that makes it inevitable. The verse thus crowns the appeal: Philemon is to receive Onesimus no longer as a mere slave but as a beloved brother in Christ — the new relation that the next verses press to its conclusion.
17:
On Philemon 17 — “If thou count me therefore a partner, receive him as myself” — the witnesses mark the height of the appeal.

The Eastern fathers note the climax. Chrysostom: after so many praises and so much preparation, “he hath introduced this great matter.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “if… thou countest me a partner” (koinonon), assumed true; “receive him as myself” — “surpassing delicacy and consummate tact.” The Reformed Calvin: Paul puts the runaway in his own room.

The verse makes the central request. If Philemon counts Paul a partner, let him receive Onesimus as Paul himself. The apostle stands in the slave’s place.

So the witnesses see Paul bring the appeal to its height: receive Onesimus as you would receive me. The witnesses note Chrysostom’s observation of the consummate preparation — only after all the praises, the tender titles, and the providential reading does Paul introduce this great request, when the heart is fully won. They mark the ground: “if thou count me therefore a partner” — if Philemon reckons Paul a partner, an associate sharing common faith, interests, and affection (a relationship assumed as true); on that partnership Paul builds. They observe the astonishing request: “receive him as myself” — let Philemon welcome the returning slave exactly as he would welcome Paul in person; the apostle identifies himself wholly with Onesimus, putting the runaway in his own place. They note Robertson’s tribute to the surpassing delicacy and tact of this — Paul cannot ask more, nor more graciously. They observe Calvin’s point that Paul lowers himself to stand in the room of a runaway, that Onesimus might have a gentle reception. The verse thus reaches the summit of the plea: by the bond of partnership, Philemon is to receive the slave as he would the apostle himself — a request so framed that refusal would wound Paul in person, and to which Paul adds his own pledge to cover any debt.

So the witnesses see Paul bring the appeal to its height: receive Onesimus as you would receive me. Chrysostom marks the consummate preparation — only after all the praises and tender titles does Paul introduce this great request, when the heart is fully won. The ground: “if thou count me therefore a partner” — if Philemon reckons Paul a partner sharing common faith and affection. The astonishing request: “receive him as myself” — welcome the returning slave exactly as Paul in person; the apostle identifies himself wholly with Onesimus. Robertson’s tribute: surpassing delicacy and tact. By the bond of partnership, refusal would wound Paul himself.
18:
On Philemon 18 — “If he hath wronged thee, or oweth thee ought, put that on mine account” — the witnesses mark Paul’s assumption of the debt.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the delicate hint. Robertson: “if he hath wronged thee at all” (first-class condition) — Onesimus “had probably robbed Philemon before he ran away”; “put that on mine account” (charge to me). Adam Clarke: “a possible case… I will discharge all he owes.” The Reformed Calvin infers Onesimus had stolen, as runaways did.

The verse offers to pay Onesimus’s debt. If the slave wronged or owes Philemon, Paul will bear the charge. The apostle becomes surety for the runaway.

So the witnesses see Paul take upon himself whatever Onesimus owed. The witnesses note the delicacy of the hypothetical form: “if he hath wronged thee, or oweth thee ought” — Paul gently couches in “if” what was likely a real wrong; the runaway had probably robbed his master before fleeing, as runaways commonly did, yet Paul softens the charge by stating it as a mere possibility. They mark the generous offer: “put that on mine account” — charge the loss or debt to Paul; the apostle makes himself surety, undertaking to pay whatever Onesimus had taken or owed. They observe that this removes the last obstacle to reconciliation: no question of money or loss need stand between master and returning slave, for Paul covers it. They note the beauty of the gospel picture many have seen here — Paul standing surety for the debtor, bidding the wronged master reckon the debt to him, a faint echo of Christ bearing the believer’s debt. They observe that Paul, who had nothing, yet pledges to repay, so earnest is his love. The verse thus completes the plea’s practical side: whatever Onesimus owes, Paul assumes it, that Philemon may receive him freely — a pledge Paul will confirm in his own hand.

So the witnesses see Paul take upon himself whatever Onesimus owed. The delicacy of the hypothetical: “if he hath wronged thee, or oweth thee ought” — Paul gently couches in “if” what was likely a real wrong; the runaway had probably robbed his master before fleeing, yet Paul softens it. The generous offer: “put that on mine account” — charge the debt to Paul, who makes himself surety. This removes the last obstacle to reconciliation: no question of money need stand between master and slave. Many have seen here a faint echo of the gospel — Paul standing surety for the debtor, as Christ bears the believer’s debt.
19:
On Philemon 19 — “I Paul have written it with mine own hand, I will repay it: albeit I do not say to thee how thou owest unto me even thine own self besides” — the witnesses mark the signed pledge and the gentle reminder.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the bond and the hint. Robertson: “I will repay” (apotiso), a note of hand; “with mine hand” (2 Thessalonians 3:17). Ellicott: “a regular bond couched in legal form.” The Reformed Calvin: he is so confident Philemon will grant it. Adam Clarke: Philemon owed Paul his very soul.

The verse gives Paul’s written guarantee and a tender reminder. He signs the pledge to repay, then recalls that Philemon owes Paul his very self. The debt of conversion outweighs any loss.

So the witnesses see Paul confirm his surety in writing, then gently turn the tables. The witnesses note the formal pledge: “I Paul have written it with mine own hand, I will repay it” — Paul puts his own signature to the promise, a legal note of hand, binding himself personally to make good Onesimus’s debt; the autograph (unusual, for he often dictated) is itself a mark of special favor and a sure bond. They mark the gracious afterthought, half-suppressed: “albeit I do not say to thee how thou owest unto me even thine own self besides” — with delicate irony Paul reminds Philemon that, while Paul offers to repay Onesimus’s debt, Philemon owes Paul a far greater one: his very self, his soul, since Philemon was converted under Paul’s ministry. They observe the tact of the “I do not say” — Paul mentions the debt precisely by professing not to mention it, so that the reminder lands without pressing. They note Calvin’s sense of Paul’s confidence: he is so sure of Philemon that he could ask anything. They observe the unanswerable logic: how can Philemon refuse to forgive a small debt to one who owes Paul his eternal life? The verse thus seals the surety and disarms all reluctance: Paul will pay what Onesimus owes, yet Philemon’s own infinite debt to Paul makes the favor a small thing to grant.
20:
On Philemon 20 — “Yea, brother, let me have joy of thee in the Lord: refresh my bowels in the Lord” — the witnesses mark the final personal entreaty.

The grammatical-historical commentators note another pun. Robertson: “let me have joy of thee” (onaimen), optative of wish, a play on Onesimus’s name. Adam Clarke and Ellicott: “let me have profit of thee” — an evident paronomasia. The word “refresh” (anapauson) echoes verse 7.

The verse repeats the appeal with a play on the name. Let Paul have joy and profit of Philemon in the Lord; let his heart be refreshed. The apostle asks for himself what he asks for Onesimus.

So the witnesses see Paul press the entreaty home once more, with a tender pun. The witnesses note the affectionate address, “yea, brother” — an urgent, loving appeal. They mark the wordplay the expositors point out: “let me have joy” or “profit” of thee — the Greek echoing the name Onesimus (“profitable”); as Onesimus is to be profitable to Philemon, so Paul asks to have profit and joy of Philemon in this very matter. They observe the request: that Philemon, by granting the favor, would give Paul gladness and benefit “in the Lord,” as a gift received from Christ. They note the closing plea: “refresh my bowels in the Lord” — deliberately echoing verse 7, where Philemon had refreshed the hearts of the saints; now Paul asks that his own heart be refreshed in the same way, by the kind reception of Onesimus. They observe how this turns Philemon’s own commended virtue into the very thing requested: the man who refreshed so many is asked to refresh Paul. The verse thus brings the personal appeal to its warm conclusion: let Philemon’s grace to Onesimus be the joy and refreshment of the aged apostle’s heart — a plea Paul closes with confident hope of even more than he asks.

So the witnesses see Paul press the entreaty home once more, with a tender pun. The affectionate “yea, brother” — an urgent, loving appeal. The wordplay: “let me have joy” or “profit” of thee — the Greek echoing the name Onesimus (“profitable”); as Onesimus is to profit Philemon, so Paul asks to have profit and joy of Philemon in this matter. The closing plea: “refresh my bowels in the Lord” — deliberately echoing verse 7, where Philemon refreshed the saints’ hearts; now Paul asks his own heart be refreshed by the kind reception of Onesimus, turning Philemon’s commended virtue into the very thing requested.
21:
On Philemon 21 — “Having confidence in thy obedience I wrote unto thee, knowing that thou wilt also do more than I say” — the witnesses mark the hint at more than is asked.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the suggestion. Robertson: “even beyond what I say” (kai hyper ha lego) — Paul “knows” Philemon will set Onesimus free, preferring it come freely. Adam Clarke: “some think the apostle hints that he should manumit Onesimus.” Barnes: more than mentioned — receiving and kindly treating him.

The verse expresses confidence in Philemon. Paul writes sure of his obedience, knowing he will do even more than asked. The hint of emancipation hovers.

So the witnesses see Paul close the appeal with confident trust and a delicate hint. The witnesses note the confidence: “having confidence in thy obedience” — Paul is sure that Philemon, out of love and Christian compliance, will grant his request; he writes not in doubt but in assurance. They mark the gracious flattery of trust: by expressing confidence, Paul binds Philemon the more surely to fulfill it — few will disappoint one who so trusts them. They observe the pregnant hint: “knowing that thou wilt also do more than I say” — Paul looks for Philemon to go beyond the explicit request. The expositors widely take this as a delicate suggestion that Philemon should not merely forgive and receive Onesimus, but set him free — Paul hinting at manumission without commanding it, leaving the generous act to Philemon’s own heart. They note Robertson’s sense that Paul “knows” Philemon will free the slave, but prefers it come as a free gift rather than a demand. They observe the perfect consistency with the whole letter’s method: never compelling, always leaving room for free, generous love. The verse thus seals the plea with trustful confidence and the unspoken hope of more — that Philemon, doing beyond what is asked, will grant Onesimus not only pardon but freedom.

So the witnesses see Paul close the appeal with confident trust and a delicate hint. The confidence: “having confidence in thy obedience” — Paul is sure Philemon will grant his request; the trust itself binds him the more surely to fulfil it. The pregnant hint: “knowing that thou wilt also do more than I say” — the expositors widely take this as a delicate suggestion that Philemon set Onesimus free, hinting at manumission without commanding it. Robertson’s sense: Paul “knows” Philemon will free the slave, but prefers it come as a free gift. Perfectly consistent with the letter’s method: never compelling, always leaving room for free, generous love.
22:
On Philemon 22 — “But withal prepare me also a lodging: for I trust that through your prayers I shall be given unto you” — the witnesses mark the hope of a visit.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the request. Robertson: “a lodging” (xenian); “I shall be granted unto you” (charisthesomai), as a favor. Gill: he hoped to come and see how Philemon received Onesimus. Adam Clarke: an obligation — he will repay by lodging with him. The Reformed Calvin: this confidence powerfully moved Philemon.

The verse adds a personal request and hope. Paul asks Philemon to prepare him a lodging, trusting to be released to them by their prayers. His expected coming reinforces the appeal.

So the witnesses see Paul, having made his plea, add a request that quietly strengthens it. The witnesses note the practical ask: “prepare me also a lodging” — let Philemon ready a guest-room, for Paul hopes to visit. They mark the spring of his hope: “I trust that through your prayers I shall be given unto you” — Paul expects, by the prayers of Philemon and the church, to be released from prison and granted to them as a favor; his coming is in God’s gift, sought through their intercession. They observe how this bears on the appeal: the prospect of Paul’s arrival would powerfully move Philemon (as Calvin notes) to deal well with Onesimus, knowing the apostle would soon come and see how the slave had been received; it is a gentle accountability. They note that Paul does not demand grand quarters but a simple lodging, content with little. They observe that whether or not Paul was in fact released and made the visit is uncertain, but the hope was real. The verse thus reinforces the plea with the promise of a visit: Philemon, expecting to host the aged apostle soon, has every reason to receive Onesimus kindly — and Paul closes the letter with the greetings of their common friends.

So the witnesses see Paul, having made his plea, add a request that quietly strengthens it. The practical ask: “prepare me also a lodging” — let Philemon ready a guest-room, for Paul hopes to visit. The spring of his hope: “I trust that through your prayers I shall be given unto you” — Paul expects, by their intercession, to be released and granted to them as a favour. This bears on the appeal: the prospect of Paul’s arrival would powerfully move Philemon (Calvin) to deal well with Onesimus, knowing the apostle would soon come and see how the slave had been received — a gentle accountability. Paul asks not grand quarters but a simple lodging.
23:
On Philemon 23 — “There salute thee Epaphras, my fellowprisoner in Christ Jesus” — the witnesses mark the greeting from Epaphras.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify him. Robertson: Epaphras, “the Colossian preacher,” who had come to Rome to enlist Paul’s help against incipient Gnosticism (Colossians 4:12); “my fellow-prisoner.” Gill: a Colossian, minister at Colosse, well known to Philemon. Adam Clarke notes he is called “one of you” in Colossians.

The verse begins the closing greetings. Epaphras, Paul’s fellow-prisoner, salutes Philemon. The friends at Rome join in the appeal.

So the witnesses see Paul append the greetings of his companions, beginning with Epaphras. The witnesses note who Epaphras was: a Colossian, the minister who had founded or led the church at Colosse (and the neighboring towns), well known to Philemon as one of his own city. They mark that he had come to Rome to seek Paul’s help against the false teaching threatening the Lycus valley, and was now with the apostle. They observe the title: “my fellowprisoner in Christ Jesus” — Epaphras shared Paul’s imprisonment, or at least his confinement, for the gospel’s sake; whether literally jailed or voluntarily sharing Paul’s captivity, he was bound up with him in the cause. They note the weight this greeting adds to the letter: Philemon’s own pastor and townsman joins in saluting him, lending his concurrence to the appeal for Onesimus. They observe that the naming of these friends, the same who appear in the Colossian letter, confirms that Philemon lived at Colosse. The verse thus opens the warm farewell: Epaphras, Philemon’s familiar minister and Paul’s fellow-prisoner, sends his greeting — the first of the companions whose united affection surrounds the appeal.

So the witnesses see Paul append the greetings of his companions, beginning with Epaphras. Who he was: a Colossian, the minister who led the church at Colosse, well known to Philemon as his townsman, who had come to Rome to seek Paul’s help against the false teaching threatening the Lycus valley. The title: “my fellowprisoner in Christ Jesus” — Epaphras shared Paul’s confinement for the gospel’s sake. This greeting adds weight to the letter: Philemon’s own pastor joins in saluting him, lending his concurrence to the appeal for Onesimus. The naming of these friends confirms that Philemon lived at Colosse.
24:
On Philemon 24 — “Marcus, Aristarchus, Demas, Lucas, my fellowlabourers” — the witnesses mark the company of fellow-workers.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify them. Robertson: the “co-workers” named in detail in Colossians 4:10–14 — Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, Luke. Gill: Mark, Barnabas’s kinsman; Aristarchus; and the rest. The Reformed Calvin notes Demas, who “afterwards forsook him” (2 Timothy 4:10).

The verse names the rest of Paul’s companions. Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke, his fellow-workers, send greetings. The whole apostolic circle joins.

So the witnesses see Paul add the greetings of his fellow-laborers, the same band named in the Colossian letter. The witnesses note the four: “Marcus” — John Mark, kinsman of Barnabas, now reconciled to Paul and useful to him; “Aristarchus” — the Thessalonian who had shared Paul’s dangers; “Demas” — here counted a fellow-worker, though, as Calvin sadly observes, he would later forsake Paul, having loved this present world; and “Lucas” — Luke, the beloved physician and evangelist, Paul’s faithful companion. They mark that all are called “my fellowlabourers,” partners in the gospel work, who join in saluting Philemon and so add their concurrence to the appeal. They observe Calvin’s sober note on Demas: even one of Paul’s assistants, here honored, afterward fell away — a warning that no one should presume too confidently on a single season of zeal. They note that the presence of these well-known names, identical to those in Colossians, again confirms the letter’s setting and authenticity. The verse thus completes the circle of greeting: the whole company of Paul’s fellow-workers, men known to Philemon, unite their affection with the apostle’s — before he closes with the benediction of grace.

So the witnesses see Paul add the greetings of his fellow-labourers, the same band named in the Colossian letter. The four: “Marcus” (John Mark, kinsman of Barnabas, now reconciled to Paul and useful to him); “Aristarchus” (the Thessalonian who shared Paul’s dangers); “Demas” (here a fellow-worker, though, as Calvin sadly observes, he would later forsake Paul, having loved this present world); and “Lucas” (Luke, the beloved physician and faithful companion). All are “fellowlabourers” who join in saluting Philemon, adding their concurrence to the appeal. Calvin’s sober warning from Demas: let no one presume too confidently on a single season of zeal.
25:
On Philemon 25 — “The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. Amen” — the witnesses mark the closing benediction.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the farewell. Robertson: “grace” (he charis), as in the greeting (v. 3), so in the farewell. Gill: not with Philemon’s spirit only, but with all addressed, hence the plural. Adam Clarke: “your” (plural) addresses the whole church in his house. The Puritan Henry: grace is the best wish, with which Paul begins and ends.

The verse closes with grace. The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with their spirit. Amen. The letter ends as it began, in grace.

So the witnesses see Paul close this little masterpiece with his customary benediction of grace. The witnesses note the symmetry: “grace” opened the letter (v. 3) and now closes it — the free favor of the Lord Jesus Christ, the sum of all blessing, with which, as Henry observes, Paul begins and ends. They mark the breadth of the wish: “be with your spirit” — the “your” is plural, so the benediction falls not on Philemon alone but on Apphia, Archippus, and the whole church in his house, all addressed at the outset. They observe that the grace is wished for the “spirit,” the inmost soul, where the believer most needs and enjoys it. They note Henry’s point that grace is the best of all wishes, for all grace is from Christ, who purchased and bestows it, and to have it is to have all that makes for happiness. They observe that this gracious close fittingly crowns a letter that has been throughout a model of grace — gracious in its appeal, gracious in its tact, gracious in its hope for the runaway. The verse thus ends the epistle where it began, in the grace of Christ: a benediction upon Philemon and his household, sealing with “Amen” the church’s assent — and leaving the case of Onesimus in the hands of the grace that reconciles.

So the witnesses see Paul close this little masterpiece with his customary benediction of grace. The symmetry: “grace” opened the letter (v. 3) and now closes it — the free favour of the Lord Jesus Christ, the sum of all blessing, with which, Henry observes, Paul begins and ends. The breadth: “be with your spirit” — the “your” is plural, so the benediction falls on Apphia, Archippus, and the whole church, not Philemon alone. The grace is wished for the “spirit,” the inmost soul. This gracious close fittingly crowns a letter throughout a model of grace — gracious in appeal, in tact, and in hope for the runaway — sealed with “Amen.”
